title: Case 85597: College Decision 050/26/COL - Closure decision - Norway - Complaint concerning the application of the requirement of comprehensive sickness insurance under Directive 2004/38
text:
Case No: 85597
Document No: 1536309
Decision No: 050/26/COL
EFTA SURVEILLANCE AUTHORITY DECISION
of 4 March 2026
closing a complaint case arising from an alleged wrong application of the requirement
of having a comprehensive sickness insurance cover under Article 7(1)(b) and (c) of
Directive 2004/38/EC
THE EFTA SURVEILLANCE AUTHORITY
Having regard to the Agreement between the EFTA States on the Establishment of a
Surveillance Authority and a Court of Justice, in particular Article 31 thereof,
Whereas:
1. Background
On 2 September 2020, the EFTA Surveillance Authority (“the Authority”) received a
complaint against Norway alleging that the Immigration Appeals Board (“UNE”) was
breaching rights under Directive 2004/38/EC (“Directive 2004/38” or “the Directive”)1, by
rejecting an application for a residence permit as a third-country national family member of
a returning Norwegian national, on the basis that she had not been covered by a
comprehensive sickness insurance in the host EEA State (Spain) during the entire period
of residence there.
In addition to scrutinising the above issue concerning Norway’s application of the sickness
insurance requirement in the so-called ‘return situation’, the Authority has also, in the
context of this case, due to an enquiry received and registered under Case 86616,
examined Norway’s application of the sickness insurance requirement more broadly,
including what is considered to constitute a ‘comprehensive sickness insurance’ within the
meaning of Article 7(1)(b) and (c) of the Directive.
After having examined the case, the Authority has reached the conclusion that the
Norwegian Government has taken the necessary measures to address the issues raised
in this case.
1 Act referred to at point 1 of Annex V to the EEA Agreement (Directive 2004/38/EC of the European
Parliament and of the Council of 29 April 2004 on the right of citizens of the Union and their family
members to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States amending Regulation
(EEC) No 1612/68 and repealing Directives 64/221/EEC, 68/360/EEC, 72/194/EEC, 73/148/EEC,
75/34/EEC, 75/35/EEC, 90/364/EEC, 90/365/EEC and 93/96/EEC) as adapted to the EEA
Agreement by Protocol 1 thereto.
Avenue des Arts 19H, B-1000 Brussels, tel: +32 2 286 18 11, www.eftasurv.int

Page 2
2. Correspondence
On 20 April 2021, the Directorate sent a request for information to Norway.2 The Norwegian
Government replied by letter dated 20 May 2021.3 In the letter, it was explained that the
Directorate of Immigration (“UDI”) did not require documentation of a family member’s
sickness insurance in the host EEA State in a return situation and that UDI had no
knowledge of any cases where an application for a residence permit had been rejected on
such grounds. The Norwegian Government, however, also noted that the Oslo District
Court had delivered a judgment in the complainant’s case on 19 April 2021,4 declaring
invalid the decision of UNE whereby the complainant, a third-country national family
member, had been required to have a sickness insurance in the host EEA State (Spain)
before returning with the Norwegian spouse to Norway.
The case was discussed at the package meeting in Oslo on 29 October 2021.5 The
Norwegian Government replied to the Authority’s follow-up letter by letter dated 7 January
2022.6 In its letter, the Norwegian Government noted that it would await the outcome of the
court proceedings in the complainant’s case before making observations in relation to the
application of the comprehensive sickness insurance requirement in the return situation.
On 5 September 2022, the Borgarting Court of Appeal upheld the District Court’s judgment
in the complainant’s case, albeit on different grounds.7 The Court of Appeal found that it
would be disproportionate to reject the complainant’s application for a residence permit,
including due to the lack of clarity in Norwegian law and inconsistencies in the practice of
the immigration authorities.
The case was discussed at the package meeting in Oslo on 28 October 2022.8 At the
meeting, the representatives of the Norwegian Government explained that, up until now, it
had only been possible to fulfil the comprehensive sickness insurance requirement in
Norway with a private sickness insurance, but that this practice was currently being looked
into, in light of recent judgments of the Court of Justice of the European Union (“CJEU”).
Norway replied to the follow-up letter by letter of 30 January 2023,9 noting that the aim was
to adopt a new Circular on the application of the comprehensive sickness insurance
requirement.
By e-mail of 3 February 2023,10 the Norwegian Government informed the Directorate that
a new Circular on the issue had been adopted on 31 January 2023,11 abolishing the general
requirement for all economically inactive EEA nationals and their family members to have
a private sickness insurance. Circular AI-1/2023 now provides that the requirement of a
comprehensive sickness insurance in Article 7(1)(b) and (c) of Directive 2004/38 will be
fulfilled by EEA nationals and their family members who are members of the National
2 Doc No 1186501.
3 Doc No 1202322.
4 Judgment of 19 April 2021 in Case 20-145340TVI-OTIR/02
5 See the Authority’s follow-up letter of 26 November 2021, Doc No 1247323.
6 Doc No 1261138.
7 Judgment of 5 September 2022 in Case 21-083091ASD-BORG/03.
8 See the Authority’s follow-up letter of 15 December 2022, Doc No 1325668.
9 Doc No 1348787.
10 Doc No 1350071.
11 Doc No Circular AI-1/2023 on the interpretation of the requirement in Section 112(1)(c) and (d)
of the Immigration Act to have a comprehensive sickness insurance.
https://www.regjeringen.no/no/dokumenter/instruks-om-tolkningen-av-vilkaret-i-utlendingsloven-
112-forste-ledd-bokstav-c-og-d-om-a-ha-en-heldekkende-sykeforsikring/id2961702/1350071.

Page 3
Insurance Scheme (Folketrygden), except during the first 12 months of residence, where a
private insurance is required.12
On 14 February 2023, the Directorate sent a supplementary request for information to
Norway, asking for clarifications on certain issues concerning the interpretation of the new
Circular, as well as inviting the Government to comment on the judgment of the Borgarting
Court of Appeal in the complainant’s case.13 Norway replied by letter dated 31 March
2023.14 The Norwegian Government confirmed that UDI does not require that a third-
country national family member of a returning Norwegian national must have been covered
by a sickness insurance in the host EEA State. The Government furthermore clarified that
the Court of Appeal concluded that there are no grounds for such a requirement in
Norwegian law. With regard to the new Circular, the Government provided clarification
concerning its interpretation and application.
A virtual meeting was held on 22 September 2023, where some remaining issues were
discussed. Following up on that meeting, a final request for information was sent to Norway
on 2 October 2023.15 The Norwegian Government replied by letter dated 24 November
2023.16
3. Assessment
Article 7(1)(b) and (c) of Directive 2004/38 makes it a requirement in order for economically
inactive EEA nationals to have a right of residence for a period of longer than three months
in a host EEA State that they, inter alia, have comprehensive sickness insurance cover in
that State. The same applies for third-country national family members of EEA nationals
under Article 7(2) of the Directive.17
It is moreover settled case law that Articles 7(1) and (2) of the Directive apply by analogy
when an EEA national returns to his/her home EEA State with a third-country national
family member after having exercised his/her right to freedom of movement in another EEA
State by genuinely residing there and entail a derived right of residence for the third-country
national family member in the EEA national’s home EEA State.18
As noted above, the Authority has examined two different issues in this case. First, the
Authority looked into Norway’s application of the sickness insurance requirement towards
third-country national family members of Norwegian nationals in the so-called ‘return
situation’. More specifically, the Authority assessed UNE’s practice of requiring third-
country national family members of Norwegian nationals to have had a comprehensive
sickness insurance cover during the period of residence in the host EEA State before
returning to Norway. Second, the Authority examined the issue of what constitutes a
comprehensive sickness insurance more broadly and Norway’s application of that
requirement towards EEA nationals and their family members.
3.1 The application of the sickness insurance requirement in the return situation
As mentioned above, the complainant in the case is a third-country national family member
of a returning Norwegian national, whose application for a residence permit in Norway was
rejected by UNE on 2 September 2020, on the grounds that she had not been covered by
12 See Section 3 of the Circular.
13 Doc No 1350454.
14 Doc No 1364228.
15 Doc No 1399653.
16 Doc No 1417094.
17 Judgment of 10 March 2022 in Case C-247/20 VI, paragraph 63.
18 See judgment of 26 July 2016 in Case E-28/15 Jabbi and judgment of 13 May 2020 in Case E-
4/19 Campbell, see also the decision of the Norwegian Supreme Court’s Grand Chamber (HR-2025-
490-S), dated 17 March 2025.

Page 4
a sickness insurance in the host EEA State (Spain) during the entire period of residence
there, before returning to Norway. Therefore, in UNE’s view, the conditions of Article 7(1)(b)
of the Directive were not fulfilled.
In addition to complaining to the Authority, the complainant brought proceedings against
the Norwegian State before domestic courts, challenging UNE’s decision of 2 September
2020. As noted above, the Oslo District Court delivered its judgment on 19 April 2021 and
declared UNE’s decision invalid, on the grounds that UNE’s practice was based on an
incorrect reading of EEA law, since there was no requirement that the third-country national
family member was previously covered by a sickness insurance in the host EEA State in
order to be granted a derived right of residence in the home EEA State.
The judgment was appealed to the Borgarting Court of Appeal, which delivered its judgment
on 5 September 2022. That judgment became final when the Supreme Court’s Appeal
Committee rejected leave to appeal on 10 November 2022.19 The Court of Appeal also
declared UNE’s decision invalid, but on different grounds than the Oslo District Court.
The Court of Appeal analysed the relevant case law of the CJEU and the EFTA Court and
concluded that under EEA law it could in principle be a requirement that the third-country
national family member had been covered by a sickness insurance in the host EEA State
during the period of residence there, in order to be granted a residence permit in Norway
upon return, pursuant to Article 7(1)(b) and (c) of the Directive. However, the Court of
Appeal also found that it would be disproportionate to reject the complainant’s application
for a number of reasons, including due to the lack of clarity in Norwegian law and
inconsistencies in the practice of the immigration authorities.
On 7 December 2022, UNE adopted a new decision in the complainant’s case,
acknowledging the complainant’s derived right of residence in Norway and stating that it
took note of the judgment of the Court of Appeal and amended the previous decision on
the basis of the Court’s conclusion.
The Authority recalls that the CJEU and the EFTA Court have dealt with the return situation
in a number of cases, i.e. where a third-country national family member of a returning EEA
national claims a derived right of residence in the EEA national’s home EEA State. In O
and B, the CJEU found that due to the clear wording and aim of Directive 2004/38, such a
residence right could not be based on the Directive but only on Article 21 TFEU.20 The
CJEU further stated that, although Directive 2004/38 was not applicable to such a situation,
the Directive should be applied by analogy and the conditions governing such a right of
residence should not be stricter than those provided for by the Directive.21The EFTA Court
reached a similar conclusion in Jabbi but solely on the basis of Directive 2004/38.22
It is furthermore settled case law of the CJEU and the EFTA Court that in order for a third-
country national family member to have a derived right of residence in the EEA national’s
home EEA State, it is a condition that the residence in the host EEA State was genuine
and that family life was created or strengthened.23
In Campbell, where the concept of genuine residence was clarified, the EFTA Court held:24
Genuine residence in the host EEA State goes hand in hand with creating and
strengthening family life in that State. As such, residence in the host EEA State
19 See the decision of the Norwegian Supreme Court's Appeals Committee (HR-2022-2165-U),
dated 10 November 2022.
20 Judgment of 12 March 2014 in Case C-456/12, O. and B.
21 Ibid, paragraph 61.
22 Jabbi, cited above, paragraph 82.
23 See e.g. O. and B., cited above, paragraph 56, and Jabbi, cited above, paragraph 80.
24 Campbell, cited above, paragraph 63.

Page 5
pursuant to and in conformity with the conditions set out in Article 7(1) and (2) of
the Directive is evidence of settling there and enables the EEA national to create or
strengthen family life.
It follows that the requirement of genuine residence entails that the residence in the host
EEA State in accordance with the conditions set out in Article 7 of the Directive is sufficient
evidence of settling and thus of genuine residence.
The Authority takes the view that it should only be relevant to assess whether the EEA
national’s residence in the host EEA State was in conformity with the conditions set out in
Article 7(1) of the Directive and whether the third-country national family member actually
resided there with the EEA national (family life created or strengthened). It would, in the
Authority’s view, be disproportionate to also require the third-country national family
member to have possessed a comprehensive sickness insurance in the host EEA State
during the period of residence there in order to establish whether the residence of the third
country family member was genuine.
This is supported by the fact that a derived right of residence for a third-country national
who is a family member of an EEA national exists only in order to ensure that the EEA
national can exercise his/her freedom of movement effectively. Thus, the focus should be
on the legal status of the EEA national in the host EEA State and whether he/she has
exercised his/her freedom of movement in accordance with EEA law and complied with the
conditions of Article 7(1) of the Directive.
This conclusion is also supported by the aim and purpose behind Article 7(1)(b) and (c) of
Directive 2004/38. The requirement for economically inactive EEA nationals to have a
comprehensive sickness insurance for them and their family members is intended to ensure
that they do not become an unreasonable burden on the public finances of that EEA
State.25 The question of whether a third-country national family member possessed a
comprehensive sickness insurance in a host EEA State before returning with an EEA
national to the home EEA State has no bearing on the public finances of the home EEA
State.
The Authority is therefore of the view that UNE’s decision in the complainant’s case of 2
September 2020 was not compatible with EEA law. However, that decision has now been
declared invalid by both the Oslo District Court and the Borgarting Court of Appeal and a
new decision was adopted by UNE on 7 December 2022.
Although the Borgarting Court of Appeal’s reasoning seems to indicate that there is a
requirement under EEA law for a third-country national family member of a returning EEA
national to have possessed a sickness insurance in the host EEA State before returning to
the home EEA State, the Court of Appeal made clear that such a conclusion would be
disproportionate in the complainant’s case, in particular due to the lack of clarity in
Norwegian law and inconsistencies in the practice of the immigration authorities. The
Norwegian Government has moreover confirmed that there are no grounds for such a
requirement in Norwegian law.26 This must be understood as meaning that rejecting an
application for a residence permit on the basis of lack of proof of comprehensive sickness
insurance of the third country family member during their stay in another EEA State would
currently not be possible in Norway, without amendments to the national rules on the
matter. In addition to this, UDI has confirmed that it does not apply this requirement in
practice and UNE reversed their decision in the complainant’s case following the judgment
of the Borgarting Court of Appeal.
It therefore appears that, in its current practice, Norway does not require third-country
national family members of returning Norwegian nationals to have had a sickness
25 See recital 10 in the preamble to Directive 2004/38.
26 Doc No 1364228.

Page 6
insurance cover in the host EEA State in order to obtain a derived right of residence in
Norway. The Authority therefore takes the view that the current practice in Norway is
compatible with EEA law and does not intend to examine the matter further.
3.2 The issue of what constitutes a comprehensive sickness insurance
The second issue examined by the Authority in this case is whether Norway’s application
of the comprehensive sickness insurance requirement is compatible with Article 7(1)(b) and
(c) of Directive 2004/38, as interpreted by the CJEU.
Section 112(1)(c) and (d) of the Norwegian Immigration Act27 implements Article 7(1)(b)
and (c) of the Directive and provides that the EEA national concerned must be covered by
a sickness insurance that covers all risks during the stay.
What constitutes a sickness insurance in Norway is further explained in UDI’s guidelines,
RUDI-2011-37,28which stipulate in Section 3.4.1 that sickness insurance is documented
through a private sickness insurance, which must cover all expenses in connection with
illness, including treatment by a doctor and possible admission to hospital.
It is also relevant to note that Section 2-1 of the National Insurance Act29 provides that
persons who reside in Norway are compulsory members of the National Insurance
Scheme. Those are considered as residing in Norway who have the intention to stay or
have stayed in Norway for at least 12 months. It is moreover a requirement for membership
in the National Insurance Scheme that the person concerned has legal residence in
Norway. Section 2-2 of the National Insurance Act furthermore provides that all workers
are obligatory members of the National Insurance Scheme.
Although Article 7(1)(b) and (c) of Directive 2004/38 makes it a requirement that EEA
nationals and their family members must be covered by a comprehensive sickness
insurance in the host EEA State, the provision does not provide guidance as regards the
type of insurance that is required. In particular, the provision does not clarify whether the
insurance contracted must be private or whether affiliation to the public health care system
of the host EEA State is sufficient.
In this context, it is useful to recall, as noted above, that the purpose of the sickness
insurance requirement is to prevent persons exercising their right of free movement from
becoming an unreasonable burden on the public finances of the host EEA State.30This was
clarified by the CJEU in Baumbast, where the Court stated that the requirement of a
comprehensive sickness insurance cover is based on the idea that the exercise of the right
of residence of EEA nationals can be subordinated to the legitimate interests of the EEA
States.31However, the CJEU also held in Baumbast that the application of the sickness
insurance requirement must be in compliance with the limits imposed by EEA law and in
particular the principle of proportionality.32
With reference to the judgment in Baumbast, the Commission stated the following
concerning the sickness insurance requirements in the reviewed guidance on the right of
free movement of EU citizens and their families:33
27 LOV-2008-05-15-35 om utlendingers adgang til riket og deres opphold her (utlendingsloven).
28 Rundskriv RUDI-2011-37.
29 LOV-1997-02-28-19 om folketrygd (folketrygdloven).
30 See recital 10 in the preamble to the Directive.
31 Judgment of 17 September 2002 in Case C-413/99, Baumbast, paragraph 90.
32 Ibid, paragraph 91.
33 Commission Notice – Guidance on the right of free movement of EU citizens and their families
(C/2023/1392), p. 34.

Page 7
Any insurance cover, private or public, contracted in the host Member State or
elsewhere, is acceptable in principle, as long as it provides comprehensive
coverage and does not create a burden on the public finances of the host Member
State. In protecting their public finances while assessing the comprehensiveness of
sickness insurance cover, Member States must act in compliance with the limits
imposed by EU law and in accordance with the principle of proportionality.
In A, the CJEU noted that, from the perspective of Article 7(1)(b) of the Directive, the
sickness insurance requirement and its purpose would be rendered redundant if it were to
be considered that the host EEA State was required to grant, to an economically inactive
EEA national residing in its territory on the basis of Article 7(1)(b), affiliation free of charge
to its public sickness insurance scheme.34 The Court therefore added the following as
regards taking affiliation to a public insurance system into account for the purposes of
Article 7(1)(b):35
In that regard, as the Advocate General stated in point 124 of his Opinion, the host
Member State may make the affiliation to its public sickness insurance system of
an economically inactive Union citizen, residing in its territory on the basis of Article
7(1)(b) of Directive 2004/38 subject to conditions intended to ensure that that citizen
does not become an unreasonable burden on the public finances of that Member
State. Such conditions may include the conclusion or maintaining by that citizen of
comprehensive private sickness insurance, enabling the reimbursement to that
Member State of the health expenses it has incurred for that citizen’s benefit, or the
payment, by that citizen, of a contribution to that Member State’s public sickness
insurance system. In that context, it is nevertheless for the host Member State to
ensure that the principle of proportionality is observed and, therefore, that it is not
excessively difficult for that citizen to comply with such conditions.
This interpretation was confirmed by the CJEU in VI, which concerned the issue whether
membership in the United Kingdom’s public sickness insurance system, offered free of
charge by the National Health Service (NHS), constituted a comprehensive sickness
insurance cover within the meaning of Article 7(1)(b) of the Directive. The CJEU concluded
the following:36
In that regard, it must be recalled that, although the host Member State may, subject
to compliance with the principle of proportionality, make affiliation to its public
sickness insurance system of an economically inactive Union citizen, residing in its
territory on the basis of Article 7(1)(b) of Directive 2004/38, subject to conditions
intended to ensure that that citizen does not become an unreasonable burden on
the public finances of that Member State, such as the conclusion or maintaining, by
that citizen, of comprehensive private sickness insurance enabling the
reimbursement to that Member State of the health expenses it has incurred for that
citizen’s benefit, or the payment, by that citizen, of a contribution to that Member
State’s public sickness insurance system […], the fact remains that, once a Union
citizen is affiliated to such a public sickness insurance system in the host Member
State, he or she has comprehensive sickness insurance within the meaning of
Article 7(1)(b).
It follows from the above that, while EEA States may require economically inactive EEA
nationals to possess a private sickness insurance and make affiliation to its public sickness
insurance systems subject to the condition of having such a private insurance, once the
EEA national is affiliated to a public sickness insurance system, he/she is considered to
have comprehensive sickness insurance within the meaning of Article 7(1)(b) or (c) of
Directive 2004/38.
34 Judgment of 15 July 2021 in Case C-535/19 A, paragraph 56.
35 Ibid, paragraph 59.
36 VI, cited above, paragraphs 68 and 69.

Page 8
The Norwegian Government adopted a new Circular on the matter on 31 January 2023.
According to Circular AI-1/2023, it is no longer a requirement for all economically inactive
EEA nationals and their family members to have – at all times- a private sickness insurance
in order to have a right of residence in Norway. Instead, it is only a requirement to have a
private sickness insurance during the first 12 months of residence, following which the
persons concerned become members of the National Insurance Scheme, which is offered
free of charge, and which is then considered to constitute a comprehensive sickness
insurance within the meaning of Article 7(1)(b) and (c) of the Directive.37
In connection with adopting the new Circular, UDI also amended its guidelines RUDI-2011-
037 in June 2023. The guidelines now clearly specify, with reference to the new Circular,
that the sickness insurance requirement can also be fulfilled through membership in the
National Insurance Scheme, which is obtained by residing in Norway for a period of 12
months.38
Given that there is no application process for becoming a member of the National Insurance
Scheme in Norway, but membership is merely assessed by the Labour and Welfare
Administration (“NAV”) when a certain benefit or a service is applied for, the Authority
understands that the Norwegian immigration authorities shall presume that economically
inactive EEA nationals and their family members will become members of the National
Insurance Scheme after 12 months of residence, in accordance with Section 2-1 of the
National Insurance Act.39Such persons would therefore be considered to have a right of
residence in Norway if they had a private insurance which was valid for a period of 12
months from the start of the residence in Norway.
Based on the CJEU’s judgments in A and VI, it seems clear that Norway is allowed to
maintain a requirement of having a private insurance for the first 12 months of residence,
following which the persons concerned become members of the National Insurance
Scheme free of charge.
There are, however, potential case scenarios where economically inactive EEA nationals
and their family members would become members of the National Insurance Scheme in
Norway before the end of the 12 months period, but nevertheless be required to have a
private insurance for the full 12 months.40This could arise if a person would apply to NAV
for a specific social security benefit in Norway before the end of the 12 months period and
would fulfil the requirement in Section 2-1 of the National Insurance Act of having an
intention to stay in Norway for at least 12 months. In these circumstances, both Section 2-
1 of the National Insurance Act (the requirement of ‘legal residence’) and the new Circular
still require the persons concerned to have a private insurance for the full 12 months
period.41
The Authority takes the view that this practice is in line with the A and VI judgments. In VI,
the CJEU confirmed that, once an EEA national is affiliated to the public sickness insurance
system of the host EEA State, he or she has a comprehensive sickness insurance within
the meaning of Article 7(1)(b) and (c) of the Directive. However, the judgment in A clarified
that EEA States can make affiliation to its public sickness insurance systems of
economically inactive EEA nationals subject to certain conditions, including having a
private sickness insurance. In the situation described above, Norway indeed makes
affiliation to the National Insurance Scheme for the persons concerned subject to the
condition of maintaining a private insurance until the end of the 12 months period, which
appears to be in line with the CJEU’s case law. In other words, Norway is allowed to make
affiliation to the National Insurance Scheme during the first 12 months of residence subject
37 See Section 3 of the Circular.
38 UDI 2011-037 (udiregelverk.no), see Section 3.4.1.
39 Doc No 1364228. See also Section 3 of the Circular.
40 See Section 2 of the Circular.
41 Ibid.

Page 9
to the condition of maintaining a private insurance until the end of that 12-months period.
That said, the Authority underlines that, it would be incompatible with EEA law if Norway
were to affiliate economically inactive EEA nationals to the National Insurance Scheme
before the expiration of the 12-months period without informing them of the additional
condition of maintaining a private insurance for that period. It would rather be
disproportionate to later claim that the persons concerned were not lawfully residing in
Norway on the grounds that they did not possess a private sickness insurance for that
period.
The Authority therefore concludes that Norway has taken the necessary measures to
address the issues raised in relation to what constitutes a comprehensive sickness
insurance and that Circular AI-1/2023 on the application of the comprehensive sickness
insurance requirement complies with EEA law, in particular Article 7(1)(b) and (c) of
Directive 2004/38, as interpreted by the CJEU.
By letter of 27 March 2025 (Doc No 1452088), the Internal Market Affairs Directorate (‘the
Directorate’) informed the complainant of its intention to propose to the Authority that the
case be closed. The complainant was invited to submit any observations on the
Directorate’s assessment of the complaint or present any new information by 27 April 2025.
The complainant did not reply to that letter.
There are, therefore, no grounds for pursuing the present case further.
The Authority observes that this conclusion will not prevent it from assessing whether the
measure implemented by the Norwegian Government in this area – and specifically, the
relevant circular – are effective, as well as whether the relevant rules prescribed therein
are being applied in practice by all relevant authorities. To this end, the Authority notes that
on 24 October 2025, it received a separate complaint case with respect to the application
of the requirement of possessing comprehensive sickness insurance under Directive
2004/38/EC (Case No 94990, see also the Authority’s Letter to Norway informing the State
of the opening of a new case on 21 November 2025 – Doc No 1575479). The Authority is
currently examining the grounds of that complaint pertaining also to the issues examined
in the present case.
HAS ADOPTED THIS DECISION:
The complaint case arising from an alleged wrong application of the requirement of having
a comprehensive sickness insurance cover under Article 7(1)(b) and (c) of Directive
2004/38/EC, is hereby closed.

Page 10
For the EFTA Surveillance Authority,
Arne Røksund Árni Páll Árnason Nuscha Wieczorek
President College Member Responsible College Member
Melpo-Menie Joséphidès
Countersigning as Director,
Legal and Executive Affairs
This document has been electronically authenticated by Arne Roeksund, Melpo-Menie
Josephides.
